topic: A student discovers their teacher follows them on social media
raw_output:
[story 1]

The notification pinged, a small, insistent vibration against the smooth glass of my phone screen. It was from Professor Albright. My stomach tightened, a familiar, dull clench that had become routine over the last few weeks. I picked up the device, the screen illuminating my face in the dim light of my dorm room. There, in the feed, was a picture of a meticulously arranged bookshelf, captioned with a quote about intellectual pursuit. Beneath it, a comment from him: "Still wrestling with Kant, I see." A slow, heavy sigh escaped my lips, barely audible above the hum of the refrigerator. I didn't swipe it away immediately. Instead, I just stared at the image, my fingers hovering over the 'mute' button, an action that felt more like acknowledging an inevitability than an attempt to stop it.

[story 2]

I watched the profile picture load. It was a slightly blurry photo taken outdoors, sunlight catching the edges of his spectacles. My breath hitched, a shallow intake of air that didn't quite reach my lungs. It was just another piece of data, another expected interaction in this strange, digital landscape of academia. I scrolled down, noting the recent posts—a travel photo here, a critique of a recent philosophical paper there. There was no shock, no sudden surge of anger. Just a settling, a quiet acceptance of the established pattern. I let my thumb rest on the screen for a long moment, the weight of the situation pressing down on my shoulders, and then, with a deliberate, almost mechanical motion, I closed the app.

[story 3]

It happened during my late-night study session. I was reviewing notes for my history seminar when the phone buzzed. It was Professor Albright. A photograph of a cityscape, overlaid with a thoughtful caption about urban decay. I didn't jump. I simply set my pen down on the desk, the small click echoing in the silence. My gaze drifted from the screen to the textbook open on my lap, the words swimming together in my vision. It was just another piece of the day’s unfolding narrative. I picked up my highlighter, the bright yellow stroke feeling strangely inadequate against the backdrop of this persistent, unspoken connection, and continued reading, the movement of my hand steady and unhurried.
